title: Growth and Development Milestones
subtitle: The nurse uses developmental milestones to assess a child and teach age-appropriate safety, knowing some variation is normal.

patient.name: Development
patient.admitDate: The nurse assesses a child's development.

labs.v: First steps, says a few words.

nursesNote: The nurse uses developmental milestones to assess a child's progress, knowing some variation is normal. By about 2 months an infant has a social smile and lifts the head; by 6 months rolls over and sits with support; by 9 months sits alone, crawls, and develops a pincer grasp with stranger anxiety; by 12 months takes first steps and says a few words; by 2 years runs and uses 2-word phrases with parallel play; by 3 years rides a tricycle and speaks in short sentences; and by 4-5 years hops or skips, draws, and dresses. The nurse tracks milestones over time and refers for evaluation if a child loses skills or misses major milestones. Safety teaching is matched to age and skills: choking and aspiration risks in infants, falls and poisoning in mobile toddlers, and age-appropriate car seats throughout.
providerOrders: Assess milestones for the child's age
Recognize that some variation is normal
Refer for evaluation if skills are lost or milestones missed
Teach age-appropriate safety (choking, falls, car seats)
Track development over time at well visits

opts.text: A social smile appears around 2 months
First steps occur around 12 months
Some variation in timing is normal
All children reach milestones at the exact same age
Losing previously gained skills is a red flag
Safety teaching is matched to age
Stranger anxiety appears in later infancy
Toddlers engage in parallel play
Missing major milestones never needs follow-up
Track milestones over time, not one visit
Choking is a key infant safety risk
Mobile toddlers need fall and poison safety

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order assessing development at a well visit

opts.text: Note the child's exact age
Compare skills to expected milestones
Ask caregivers about progress over time
Identify any red flags or delays
Teach age-appropriate safety
Social smile and rolling over
Runs and uses 2-word phrases
Sits alone and crawls
Rides a tricycle
Develops a pincer grasp
Hops on one foot and draws

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A 12-month-old is not yet doing expected skills

opts.text: Confirm the child's exact age
Assess current skills against milestones
Ask caregivers about progress over time
Report concerns and refer for evaluation
Document and plan follow-up
Expecting a social smile around 2 months
Expecting a newborn to walk
Knowing some milestone variation is normal
Ignoring loss of previously gained skills
Teaching choking safety for infants
Skipping car-seat teaching for toddlers
Referring a child who misses major milestones
Expecting a 2-year-old to read
